meaning_english:
• **Finite Verbs:** Change according to the subject, number, and tense (e.g., He *plays*, They *play*).
• **Non-Finite Verbs:** Do not change (Infinitives, Gerunds, and Participles). Example: I like *to swim*, They like *to swim*.

• **Transitive Verbs:** Need an object to complete the sense (e.g., He *killed* a snake).
• **Intransitive Verbs:** Do not need an object (e.g., The sun *shines*, She *laughed*).

• **Stative Verbs:** Describe a state or feeling (Love, Know, Believe, Smell, Hear). These are generally NOT used in continuous (-ing) forms.
• **Rule:** Don't say 'I am knowing him'. Say 'I know him'.

• **Rule:** Singular subjects take singular verbs; plural subjects take plural verbs.
• **Exception:** Words like 'News', 'Mathematics', 'Economics' look plural but take a singular verb (e.g., The news *is* true).

• **Rule:** 'One of' is followed by a plural noun but a **singular verb**.
• Example: One of my friends *is* a doctor. (Focus is on 'one', not 'friends').

• **Lie vs Lay:** 'Lie' means to rest (Lie-Lay-Lain). 'Lay' means to put something down (Lay-Laid-Laid).
• **Rise vs Raise:** 'Rise' is intransitive (The sun rises). 'Raise' is transitive (Raise your hand).

Used to express ability, possibility, or permission.
• **Rule:** Modals are always followed by the base form of the verb (V1) without 'to'.
• Correct: He can *play*. (Incorrect: He can to play).

Used when the subject doesn't do the action itself but causes someone else to do it.
• **Make:** Force someone (He made me *cry*).
• **Get:** Persuade someone (I got him *to wash* my car).

• **Gerund:** V + ing acting as a noun (e.g., *Smoking* is injurious).
• **Infinitive:** to + V1 (e.g., I want *to study*).
• **Rule:** Certain verbs like 'Enjoy', 'Avoid', 'Finish' are followed by Gerunds.

• **Type 1:** If he *comes*, I *will go*.
• **Type 2 (Hypothetical):** If I *were* a bird, I *would fly*.
• **Type 3 (Past Unreal):** If he *had studied*, he *would have passed*.
